properties.mechanism: Copy length is a controllable creative attribute that changes ad engagement and CTR, which in turn affects a creative's measured CTR/ROAS and its ranking among top creatives.
The amount and framing of ad copy preceding a call-to-action changes how many users read through and click, affecting clicks and conversions tallied per CTA type.
Both segment CTR by a creative attribute (copy length vs. creative format) and tend to co-move because format choice and typical copy length are tested together on the same creatives.
A more compelling CTA button raises the click-through and conversion rates of the ad creative, which directly lifts the CTR, CPC and ROAS reported for each top creative.
Conversions counted per CTA type and conversions split by click/view attribution windows draw on the same underlying conversion events, so the two metrics co-move.
Both track the same daily Meta spend and CTR series (creative-level vs account-level), so they reliably co-move day to day.
Daily creative spend and CTR move together with the spend and CTR of the top creatives that dominate the daily aggregates.
Creative fatigue from repeated audience exposure causes daily CTR to decline over time, which the daily trend captures.
Rising ad frequency causes audience saturation that pushes daily CTR down.
The length of ad copy changes how compelling the creative is, driving the daily CTR up or down.
The creative format (video vs image vs link) materially affects click-through rate and therefore daily CTR.
The CTA button choice drives how many clicks a creative earns, influencing the daily CTR.
As ad frequency rises, the same users see the creative repeatedly and stop clicking, driving the CTR decline that the Fatigue Score measures.
Creatives concentrated in high-frequency buckets accumulate exposure-driven fatigue, lowering current CTR relative to initial CTR.
Both metrics classify creatives/ad sets into Healthy/Warning/Fatigue tiers from CTR decline, so their fatigue flags reliably co-move.
The daily CTR series this metric tracks is the same signal whose downward drift the Fatigue Score quantifies, so the two move together over a creative's life.
Both metrics aggregate the same per-format spend data (SUM(spend) per format), so format spend and the format summary's total spend move together.
As creatives within a format fatigue, their CTR decays, which drags down the aggregate Format CTR measured by the subject.
Both draw on the same creative-level spend, clicks, and impressions, with the subject slicing by format and this metric slicing the same data by day.
Both are creative-attribute CTR breakdowns over the same impression/click pool, so CTR shifts by format tend to co-move with CTR shifts by copy length.
Both segment the same creative click data by a creative attribute (format vs CTA), so their click/CTR patterns statistically co-move.
Top creatives by spend share the same underlying spend and CTR measures that the subject aggregates up to the format level.
Both metrics aggregate the same Meta creative data by format dimension, so the subject's per-format Total Spend co-moves directly with this metric's Format Spend.
Both count distinct Meta creative assets, so the subject's per-format Creative Count rises and falls with the overall active asset counts in this library metric.
As a top creative fatigues its CTR declines, dragging down the ROAS/CTR/CPC performance shown for that creative.
The creative format (video/image/link) drives engagement and spend allocation, shaping which creatives rank top by spend and their CTR.
Daily creative spend and CTR co-move with top-creative spend and CTR since both summarize the same underlying creative results.
The call-to-action button type drives how many users complete conversions, contributing to the click- and view-attributed actions counted in the breakdown.
The attributed conversion actions generate the omni_purchase revenue that forms the numerator of ROAS, so more actions raise ROAS.
Attributed conversion actions produce the purchase revenue that feeds each creative's ROAS, linking action volume to creative efficiency.
Daily attributed conversion actions generate the daily revenue used in the daily retargeting ROAS calculation.
Ad sets roll up into campaigns, so the identical CPM/CTR/CPC metrics at ad-set and campaign level reliably co-move.
The daily trend is the same spend/impression/click CPM and CTR metrics aggregated over time, so it tracks ad-set performance.
As creative fatigue rises, audiences stop responding to the ad, dragging down the ad set's CTR and pushing up its CPC.
Higher ad frequency causes audience saturation that suppresses CTR and therefore raises the ad set's CPC.
The creative format (video/image/link) served in an ad set drives differences in its CTR and CPM.
The CTA button used drives click volume, shaping the ad set's CTR and CPC.
Ad copy length affects click-through behavior, influencing the ad set's CTR.
Both partition the same Total Spend denominator into demographic share-of-spend buckets, so age and gender spend distributions co-move as targeting shifts.
Age composition is correlated with device mix because younger audiences skew toward mobile, so age spend shares and device spend shares co-move.
Different age groups concentrate on different placements (e.g., younger users on Instagram), so age spend distribution statistically tracks platform spend distribution.
Reallocating spend toward better-performing age groups changes the campaign-level blended CTR, CPC, and CPM outcomes.
Campaign-level CPM, CTR and CPC are aggregated from the spend, impressions and clicks of the campaign's constituent ad sets, which share the identical formula.
Campaign performance metrics are computed from the same spend, impressions and clicks summed across daily buckets, so the daily trend is the time-grain decomposition of campaign performance.
As ad creative fatigues, audience CTR declines, which lowers the CTR component and raises the effective CPC of campaign performance.
Rising ad frequency causes audience saturation that drives CTR down, degrading the campaign performance metrics.
The creative format used (video vs image vs link) changes click-through and impression behavior, driving the campaign's CTR and CPM.
A larger pool of active creative assets to rotate lets advertisers refresh ads before they wear out, lowering the CTR-decay fatigue score.
Both count distinct creative assets, so the per-format creative counts move together with the total asset library size.
Both track the same account daily spend and CTR time series, just sliced overall vs. by creative, so they move together day over day.
Retargeting daily spend is a subset of total daily spend, so the two daily trends co-move.
Creative fatigue erodes click-through rate over time, pulling down the CTR tracked in the daily performance trend.
Rising ad frequency causes audience saturation that lowers CTR, one of the daily trend's component metrics.
Both are Meta overview spend-share breakdowns of the same total spend pool, so device spend share and platform spend share co-move as budget is reallocated across delivery dimensions.
Age Demographics uses the identical spend-share and CTR-by-segment structure on the same Meta impressions/clicks data, so device-level and age-level CTR and spend shares statistically co-move.
Gender Distribution is a parallel spend-share breakdown of the same total Meta spend, so its shares co-move with device spend shares as budget shifts across audience cuts.
Region Performance partitions the same Meta spend, clicks, and impressions by geography just as Device Performance does by device, so their spend and CTR figures statistically co-move.

Device-level engagement patterns (mobile vs desktop) tend to move together across ad platforms, so Meta device CTR co-moves with Google/YouTube device view rates even though they are different platforms.
Both are spend-share breakdowns of the same Meta Total Spend denominator across demographic dimensions, so the two distributions co-move as budget is allocated.
Device Spend Share and Gender Spend Share partition the identical Meta Total Spend, so shifts in one allocation are reflected in the other.
Platform Share and Gender Spend Share are parallel decompositions of the same Meta Total Spend, causing the spend distributions to co-move.
Both partition the same Meta Total Spend denominator into share percentages, so the platform spend distribution and gender spend distribution co-move as overall budget allocation shifts.
Device Spend Share and Platform Spend Share are sibling distribution metrics built on the identical (Segment Spend / Total Spend) structure, so they move together as Meta spend is reallocated.
Age Spend Share and Platform Spend Share share the same Total Spend base and reflect the same budget-allocation decisions across different breakdown dimensions, so they statistically co-move.
Meta's platform spend distribution feeds into the blended channel spend-share allocation, so shifts in Meta platform spend co-move with the channel-level spend share.
Both partition the same total Meta ad spend along orthogonal dimensions (geography vs device) using identical spend-share and CTR formulas, so they co-move with aggregate spend.
Age and region breakdowns both cut the same total Meta spend, clicks, and impressions, so their spend-share and CTR figures statistically co-move as overall budget changes.
Gender and region are sibling spend-share breakdowns of the same Meta total spend, so they co-move as the aggregate budget shifts.
Platform and region distributions both allocate the same total Meta spend across orthogonal axes, so the underlying spend co-moves between the two breakdowns.
Region performance and the daily trend draw from the same underlying Meta spend, clicks, and impressions cut by geography vs time, so their spend and CTR move together.
The subject's status CASE is computed directly from Frequency and CTR, both of which are defined by this Frequency vs CTR metric (Frequency = Impressions/Reach, CTR = Clicks/Impressions).
Ad sets whose Frequency distribution shifts above 3-5 co-move with the subject's status degrading toward Warning/Fatigue, since both are driven by the same rising Frequency.
High audience overlap between ad sets prompts marketers to exclude overlapping custom audiences, raising the subject's exclusion rate.
Both metrics are computed over the same pool of active retargeting ad sets, so custom-audience usage levels co-move with ad-set performance counts.
When the same audiences are shared across multiple ad sets, the same users are served ads from each overlapping ad set, driving up impression frequency.
Higher audience overlap concentrates impressions on shared users, shifting the frequency distribution toward higher buckets.
Overlapping audiences compete against each other in Meta's auction, raising costs and cannibalizing conversions, which lowers ROAS for the affected audience types.
The more ad sets each custom audience is used in (higher usage rate), the more likely two audiences share ad sets, increasing the overlap count.
Audience overlap inflates frequency on shared ad sets, pushing them past the frequency thresholds that flag Warning/Fatigue status in the retargeting performance check.
Healthier retargeting ad sets (low frequency, high CTR) convert audiences more efficiently, increasing revenue per dollar of spend and thus the audience ROAS.
Lower CPC/CPM and higher CTR at the ad-set level deliver more clicks and conversions per unit of spend, mechanically lifting the revenue-over-spend ratio.
Creative fatigue erodes CTR, reducing conversions and revenue per dollar spent, which drags down the audience ROAS.
Both are Meta revenue/spend ROAS measures over the same campaigns, so creative-level ROAS and audience-level ROAS reliably co-move.
Meta conversion value and spend are components of the blended ROAS, so the Meta audience ROAS moves together with the blended figure.
The subject's Daily Spend = SUM(daily ad set spend), so the retargeting ad set performance data is the per-ad-set component that aggregates into the daily trend.
Both compute ROAS = Revenue/Spend over the same retargeting campaigns, so the daily ROAS trend and the per-audience ROAS move together as different slices of the same spend and revenue.
Rising frequency on small retargeting audiences erodes CTR via ad fatigue, lowering conversions and dragging down the daily ROAS trend.
Overlapping retargeting audiences cause the same users to be served repeatedly, inflating frequency and competition that degrade retargeting efficiency and ROAS over time.
Both metrics are derived from the same per-ad-set Frequency = Impressions/Reach value, so the subject's frequency and the bucketed distribution co-move directly.
The subject buckets the Frequency value (Impressions / Reach) that this metric computes, so that Frequency figure is the direct input to the distribution.
Rising frequency in the distribution pushes ad sets across the frequency thresholds that flip their status from Healthy to Warning to Fatigue.
